---
title: "Why Maximum Likelihood Works: Consistency, Efficiency, and Asymptotic Normality"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20240809)

DATA   = "#2b3a67"     # data / samples / empirical
CURVE  = "#c1121f"     # the theoretical limit / fitted curve
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # the MLE / the truth / highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded areas / histograms
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the keystone of the **Likelihood Spine**. It
> assumes [INFR-B2 *Maximum Likelihood*], which defined the MLE $\hat\theta_n$ as the
> argmax of the log-likelihood $\ell_n(\theta)=\sum_{i=1}^n\log f(x_i\mid\theta)$ and the
> **score** $U_n(\theta)=\ell_n'(\theta)$ whose root we chase; [INFR-B3 *Fisher
> Information*], which defined the per-observation information $I_1(\theta)$ two equivalent
> ways — as the variance of the score, $I_1(\theta)=\operatorname{Var}_\theta[\partial_\theta
> \log f(X\mid\theta)]$, and as the negative expected curvature, $I_1(\theta)=-\,\mathbb
> E_\theta[\partial_\theta^2\log f(X\mid\theta)]$ — proved their equivalence, showed
> information adds over i.i.d. data ($I_n=nI_1$), and stated the **Cramér–Rao lower bound**
> $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta)\ge 1/I_n(\theta)$ for the precision of any unbiased
> estimator; and [INFR-A2 *LLN and CLT*], whose **Law of Large Numbers** and **Central
> Limit Theorem** are the two engines we feed the score into. From outside the track it
> borrows **Taylor's theorem** with remainder (Calc-1) and **convergence in probability /
> in distribution** with **Slutsky's theorem** [PROB-21]. It **sets up** [INFR-C3 *Wald,
> Score, and Profile Intervals*], [INFR-D3 *The Asymptotic Trinity*], and the asymptotic
> inference the entire Regression track relies on.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

[INFR-B2] gave us an estimator — believe the parameter that best explains the data — but
not a reason to trust it. This article supplies the reason. We prove three facts about the
maximum-likelihood estimator $\hat\theta_n$ as the sample grows. **Consistency:**
$\hat\theta_n$ converges in probability to the true parameter $\theta_0$, argued by feeding
the Law of Large Numbers into the log-likelihood so the sample's average log-density homes
in on a curve whose unique peak sits at the truth. **Asymptotic normality:** Taylor-expanding
the score about $\theta_0$, dividing the linear pieces, and applying the Central Limit
Theorem to the numerator and the Law of Large Numbers to the denominator yields
$$
\sqrt n\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\;I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}\bigr),
$$
every step on the page. **Asymptotic efficiency:** the limiting variance $I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}$
is exactly the Cramér–Rao floor of [INFR-B3], so no regular estimator can beat the MLE in
the limit. We then derive the **delta method** — how the limiting distribution transforms
under a smooth function $g$, giving $\sqrt n(g(\hat\theta_n)-g(\theta_0))\xrightarrow{d}
\mathcal N(0, g'(\theta_0)^2 I_1(\theta_0)^{-1})$ — which turns one asymptotic result into
standard errors for odds, rates, ratios, and anything else we estimate by transforming the
MLE. Finally we mark the **edge of applicability** honestly: small $n$, parameters on a
boundary, and misspecified models, where one or more of the guarantees quietly fails. The
single claim the article defends is that **the asymptotic optimality of maximum likelihood
is not folklore — it is a theorem with a small, checkable set of hypotheses**, and knowing
those hypotheses is what separates quoting "$\hat\theta\pm 1.96\,\mathrm{SE}$" from knowing
when that interval is a lie.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Under regularity conditions (a smooth log-likelihood, an interior true parameter, a
parameter-independent support), the maximum-likelihood estimator from $n$ i.i.d. observations
is, as $n\to\infty$:

1. **Consistent** — it converges in probability to the true value:
   $\hat\theta_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta_0$.
2. **Asymptotically normal** — its error, magnified by $\sqrt n$, is Gaussian:
   $$
   \sqrt n\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\ I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}\bigr),
   $$
   so for large $n$, $\hat\theta_n \approx \mathcal N\!\bigl(\theta_0,\ \tfrac1{nI_1(\theta_0)}\bigr)$,
   and the standard error is $1/\sqrt{n I_1(\theta_0)} = 1/\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}$.
3. **Asymptotically efficient** — that limiting variance is the **smallest possible**: it
   equals the Cramér–Rao bound $I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}$, so no regular estimator beats the MLE in
   the limit.

And for any smooth transformation $g$, the **delta method** carries the result along:
$\sqrt n\,(g(\hat\theta_n)-g(\theta_0))\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\,g'(\theta_0)^2
I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}\bigr)$. The single picture to hold: **the more sharply the log-likelihood
curves at its peak (the larger the Fisher information), the more tightly the MLE concentrates
on the truth.**
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The three properties answer, in order, the three questions
a careful person asks of any estimator: *Does it get the right answer eventually?* (yes —
consistency). *How wrong is it for finite data, and what shape is the error?* (Gaussian, with
spread $1/\sqrt{nI_1}$ — asymptotic normality). *Could a cleverer estimator do better?* (no,
not in the limit — efficiency). The delta method is the lever that turns one hard-won
limiting distribution into an entire toolbox of standard errors. We will keep returning to
the one mechanical image that drives the whole derivation: **the MLE is the root of the
score, and the score is a sum of i.i.d. terms — so the LLN and the CLT, the two theorems of
[INFR-A2], are exactly the tools the problem hands us.**

# Why we are doing this: from "an estimate" to "a trustworthy estimate" {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **why should we believe the MLE, and what
exactly are we entitled to say about it?** Every derivation below answers a piece of that.

## The problem the previous articles left open

[INFR-B2] gave us a *procedure* — maximize the likelihood — and a stack of closed forms: the
proportion is the sample fraction, the rate is the sample average, the Normal mean is the
sample mean. But a procedure is not a justification. It even handed us a warning shot: the
MLE of the Normal variance is *biased* (it divides by $n$, not $n-1$), so maximum likelihood
does not even promise to be right on average for finite samples. Why, then, is it the default
estimator in nearly all of statistics — the one all of Regression silently fits?

[INFR-B3] sharpened the question without answering it. It defined **Fisher information**
$I_1(\theta)$ — how much one observation tells you about $\theta$ — and proved the
**Cramér–Rao lower bound**: *no* unbiased estimator can have variance below $1/I_n(\theta)$.
That bound is a floor on the achievable precision of any estimator. The obvious question it
raises is the one this article settles: **does the MLE actually reach that floor?** If it
does, then maximum likelihood is not merely natural — it is, in a precise sense, the best you
can do.

## Why "as $n\to\infty$" is the right lens, and its honest cost

There is a reason all three guarantees are *asymptotic* — statements about the limit
$n\to\infty$ — rather than exact finite-sample facts. For a handful of lucky models (the
Normal mean) the MLE has a clean exact sampling distribution; for almost every other model
(the Bernoulli proportion's $\hat p=k/n$ has a *discrete* distribution, logistic regression
has no closed form at all) the exact distribution of $\hat\theta_n$ is intractable. The
asymptotic theory is what makes inference *possible at all* for the general MLE: it says that
however ugly the finite-$n$ distribution, it is *approximately* Gaussian with a known center
and a known spread, and the approximation improves as data accumulate. This is the same
bargain [INFR-A2] struck for the sample mean — and it inherits the same caveat, stated
bluntly here and revisited in @sec-failure: **"asymptotic" is a promise about large $n$, and
for small $n$, skewed likelihoods, or boundary parameters the promise can be badly broken.**
The whole value of the theory is knowing both the promise and its fine print.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why do we care about asymptotic properties at all — we never have infinite data?"* Two
honest reasons. First, the asymptotic distribution is the *only* tractable handle on the
sampling distribution of a general MLE; the exact distribution is unavailable, so the Gaussian
approximation is what we actually compute confidence intervals and tests from ([INFR-C3],
[INFR-D3]). Second, "asymptotic" is shorthand for "the regime where the approximation is good
enough," and for many well-behaved models that regime arrives quickly — a few dozen
observations. The danger, which a sharp interviewer is probing, is treating the approximation
as exact when $n$ is small or the model is near a boundary. The right answer names both the
power and the precondition: the theory gives you a usable error bar *when its regularity
conditions hold and $n$ is large enough*, and a graduate-level command means being able to
say when that is.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Two pictures carry the entire article. Take them one at a time.

## Consistency: the expected log-likelihood peaks at the truth

The MLE maximizes the *observed* log-likelihood $\frac1n\ell_n(\theta)=\frac1n\sum_i\log
f(x_i\mid\theta)$, which is an average of i.i.d. terms. By the Law of Large Numbers, that
average converges to its expectation, $\mathbb E_{\theta_0}[\log f(X\mid\theta)]$ — a *fixed*
function of $\theta$ that no longer depends on the random sample. If that limiting function
has its unique maximum exactly at the true $\theta_0$, then the peak of the wiggly observed
curve must, as $n$ grows, be dragged toward the peak of the smooth limiting curve — and so
$\hat\theta_n\to\theta_0$. @fig-consistency-intuition shows the wiggly curves settling onto
the limit, their peaks marching toward the truth.

```{python}
#| label: fig-consistency-intuition
#| fig-cap: 'Why the MLE is consistent. Each thin grey curve is the *normalized* log-likelihood $\frac1n\ell_n(\lambda)$ for one Poisson sample of size $n$ (true rate $\lambda_0=3$); we draw several at $n=10$ (light) and several at $n=200$ (dark). As $n$ grows, the Law of Large Numbers pins each random curve onto the single smooth limiting curve $\mathbb E[\log f(X\mid\lambda)]$ (red), whose unique peak sits exactly at the truth $\lambda_0=3$ (green). Because the peaks of the random curves track the peak of the limit, the MLE $\hat\lambda_n$ is squeezed onto $\lambda_0$.'
lam0 = 3.0
lam = np.linspace(1.0, 6.0, 400)

def norm_loglik_poisson(counts, lam):
    # (1/n) * sum [ x log lam - lam - log x! ]; drop the constant -E[log x!] for shape
    S = counts.sum(); n = len(counts)
    return (S * np.log(lam) - n * lam) / n

# Limiting curve: E[log f(X|lam)] with X~Poisson(lam0), up to the same constant
# E[X] = lam0, so limit shape = lam0 * log(lam) - lam
limit_curve = lam0 * np.log(lam) - lam

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for nn, col, a in [(10, GREY, 0.45), (200, DATA, 0.55)]:
    for _ in range(6):
        c = rng.poisson(lam0, size=nn)
        ax.plot(lam, norm_loglik_poisson(c, lam), color=col, lw=1.0, alpha=a)
ax.plot(lam, limit_curve, color=CURVE, lw=2.6,
        label=r"limit $\mathbb{E}[\log f(X\mid\lambda)]$")
ax.axvline(lam0, color=ACCENT, lw=1.8, label=r"truth $\lambda_0=3$")
ax.plot([], [], color=GREY, lw=1.0, label="random curves, $n=10$")
ax.plot([], [], color=DATA, lw=1.0, label="random curves, $n=200$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"candidate rate $\lambda$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\frac{1}{n}\ell_n(\lambda)$  (up to a constant)")
ax.set_title("Random log-likelihoods settle onto a limit peaked at the truth")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9, loc="lower center")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Asymptotic normality: the score is a sum, so the CLT applies

The MLE solves the score equation $U_n(\hat\theta_n)=0$. The score $U_n(\theta)=\sum_i
\partial_\theta\log f(x_i\mid\theta)$ is a *sum of i.i.d. terms*, and we know two things about
each term at the truth $\theta_0$, both proved in [INFR-B3]: its mean is **zero** (the score
has expectation zero at the true parameter) and its variance is the Fisher information
$I_1(\theta_0)$. A sum of i.i.d. mean-zero, finite-variance terms is exactly what the Central
Limit Theorem was built for. So the score at the truth, suitably scaled, is asymptotically
Gaussian — and a one-step Taylor expansion transfers that Gaussianity from the score (which
we understand) to the estimator $\hat\theta_n$ (which we want). @fig-an-intuition previews the
punchline: the histogram of $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)$ over many samples settles onto a
Gaussian whose width is set by $1/\sqrt{I_1}$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-an-intuition
#| fig-cap: 'Why the MLE is asymptotically normal. For the same Poisson model ($\lambda_0=3$), we draw $30{,}000$ samples of size $n=80$, compute the MLE $\hat\lambda_n=\bar x$ on each, and histogram the rescaled error $\sqrt n(\hat\lambda_n-\lambda_0)$ (blue). It is closely matched by the Gaussian $\mathcal N(0, I_1(\lambda_0)^{-1})$ predicted by the theorem (red), where for the Poisson $I_1(\lambda)=1/\lambda$, so the limiting variance is $\lambda_0=3$. The MLE''s error is Gaussian with a variance the Fisher information dictates — before we have proved a thing.'
lam0 = 3.0; n = 80; reps = 30000
samples = rng.poisson(lam0, size=(reps, n))
lamhat = samples.mean(axis=1)
z = np.sqrt(n) * (lamhat - lam0)

# Poisson Fisher info I_1(lam) = 1/lam  ->  asymptotic variance = lam0
asym_var = lam0
grid = np.linspace(z.min(), z.max(), 300)
gauss = np.exp(-grid**2 / (2*asym_var)) / np.sqrt(2*np.pi*asym_var)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(z, bins=60, density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.75,
        label=r"empirical $\sqrt{n}(\hat\lambda_n-\lambda_0)$")
ax.plot(grid, gauss, color=CURVE,
        label=fr"$\mathcal{{N}}(0,\,I_1^{{-1}}={asym_var:.0f})$ (theory)")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\sqrt{n}\,(\hat\lambda_n - \lambda_0)$")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Rescaled MLE error is Gaussian, with variance $1/I_1$")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The rest of @sec-formal makes both pictures rigorous, in order: first the two score facts we
keep invoking (recalled from [INFR-B3] and re-derived just enough to be self-contained), then
consistency, then the Taylor-expansion-plus-CLT argument for normality, then efficiency, then
the delta method.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## Setup, notation, and the regularity conditions {#sec-setup}

We observe $X_1,\dots,X_n$ i.i.d. with density or mass function $f(x\mid\theta)$, and we write
$\theta_0$ for the **true** parameter — the one that actually generated the data. (We treat
the scalar case $\theta\in\mathbb R$ throughout the main derivations; the vector case replaces
derivatives by gradients, $I_1$ by the Fisher information *matrix*, and "$<0$" by "negative
definite," and we flag where that is the only change.) As in [INFR-B2],
$$
\ell_n(\theta)=\sum_{i=1}^n\log f(x_i\mid\theta),
\qquad
U_n(\theta)=\ell_n'(\theta)=\sum_{i=1}^n \underbrace{\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log f(x_i\mid\theta)}_{=\,u(x_i;\,\theta)},
$$ {#eq-score-sum}
where we name the **per-observation score** $u(x;\theta)=\partial_\theta\log f(x\mid\theta)$,
so $U_n(\theta)=\sum_i u(x_i;\theta)$ is literally a sum of i.i.d. random variables — the fact
that lets the CLT in. The MLE $\hat\theta_n$ is the interior solution of $U_n(\hat\theta_n)=0$.

The theorems below are not unconditional; they hold under a standard list of **regularity
conditions**, and a graduate-level command means knowing the list, because every entry is a
place the theory can fail (@sec-failure). We state them plainly and flag, for each, the step
that uses it.

::: {.callout-note title="The regularity conditions (and what each one buys)"}
1. **Identifiability.** Distinct parameters give distinct distributions: $\theta\ne\theta_0
   \Rightarrow f(\cdot\mid\theta)\ne f(\cdot\mid\theta_0)$. *Used in consistency* — it is what
   makes the limiting log-likelihood's peak at $\theta_0$ **unique**.
2. **Common support.** The set $\{x:f(x\mid\theta)>0\}$ does **not** depend on $\theta$.
   *Used everywhere* — it is what lets us differentiate under the integral sign and is exactly
   what the uniform $[0,\theta]$ model of [INFR-B2] violated.
3. **Smoothness.** $\log f(x\mid\theta)$ is twice (ideally thrice) differentiable in $\theta$.
   *Used in the Taylor expansion* of the score.
4. **Interior truth.** $\theta_0$ lies in the *interior* of the parameter space $\Theta$, not
   on its boundary. *Used so that $U_n(\hat\theta_n)=0$* — an interior maximizer has zero score.
5. **Differentiation under the integral.** We may swap $\partial_\theta$ and $\int dx$, so that
   $\mathbb E_\theta[u(X;\theta)]=0$ and $\operatorname{Var}_\theta[u(X;\theta)]=I_1(\theta)$
   (the two score identities of [INFR-B3]). *Used to feed the CLT.*
6. **Positive, finite information.** $0<I_1(\theta_0)<\infty$. *Used to divide by it* in the
   normality argument and to have a finite limiting variance.

These are the conditions under which the MLE is "regular." When they hold, the three
properties follow. When one breaks, exactly one of the properties typically breaks with it —
which is why the list is worth memorizing.
:::

## The two score identities, recalled {#sec-score-facts}

The asymptotic-normality argument leans on two facts from [INFR-B3] about the
per-observation score $u(X;\theta)=\partial_\theta\log f(X\mid\theta)$, evaluated at the true
parameter $\theta_0$. They are the bridge between "the MLE is a root of the score" and "the
CLT applies," so we recall them and re-derive them in two lines each, both relying on
regularity condition 5 (differentiation under the integral). Write $\int$ for a sum in the
discrete case throughout.

::: {#lem-score-mean}
**Lemma (the score has mean zero at the truth).** $\mathbb E_{\theta_0}[\,u(X;\theta_0)\,]=0.$
:::

::: {.proof}
Start from the fact that a density integrates to one *for every* $\theta$:
$\int f(x\mid\theta)\,dx = 1$. The right-hand side is constant in $\theta$, so its derivative
is zero; differentiate the left side under the integral (condition 5):
$$
0 = \frac{d}{d\theta}\int f(x\mid\theta)\,dx
  = \int \frac{\partial f(x\mid\theta)}{\partial\theta}\,dx .
$$
Now use the **log-derivative trick**, the single identity this whole subsection runs on. By
the chain rule, $\partial_\theta\log f = \frac{\partial_\theta f}{f}$, hence
$\partial_\theta f = f\cdot\partial_\theta\log f = f\cdot u(x;\theta)$. Substitute:
$$
0 = \int f(x\mid\theta)\,u(x;\theta)\,dx = \mathbb E_\theta[\,u(X;\theta)\,].
$$
This holds at every $\theta$, in particular at $\theta_0$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {#lem-score-var}
**Lemma (the score's variance is the Fisher information).** With $I_1(\theta)=\operatorname{Var}_\theta
[u(X;\theta)]$, we also have $I_1(\theta)=-\,\mathbb E_\theta[\,\partial_\theta u(X;\theta)\,]
= -\,\mathbb E_\theta[\,\partial_\theta^2\log f(X\mid\theta)\,]$. In particular
$\operatorname{Var}_{\theta_0}[u(X;\theta_0)]=I_1(\theta_0)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
By @lem-score-mean the score has mean zero, so its variance equals its second moment,
$\operatorname{Var}_\theta[u]=\mathbb E_\theta[u^2]$ — this defines $I_1(\theta)$. For the
"negative expected curvature" form, differentiate the mean-zero identity
$\int f(x\mid\theta)\,u(x;\theta)\,dx=0$ once more in $\theta$ (again under the integral, and
using the product rule on $f\cdot u$):
$$
0 = \frac{d}{d\theta}\int f\,u\,dx
  = \int \bigl(\partial_\theta f\bigr)u\,dx + \int f\bigl(\partial_\theta u\bigr)\,dx .
$$
In the first integral substitute $\partial_\theta f = f\,u$ (the log-derivative trick again),
turning it into $\int f\,u^2\,dx=\mathbb E_\theta[u^2]$. The second integral is
$\mathbb E_\theta[\partial_\theta u]$. So
$$
0 = \mathbb E_\theta[u^2] + \mathbb E_\theta[\partial_\theta u]
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\mathbb E_\theta[u^2] = -\,\mathbb E_\theta[\partial_\theta u],
$$
and since $\partial_\theta u = \partial_\theta^2\log f$ and the left side is
$I_1(\theta)$, both forms agree. This **information equality** — variance of the score equals
negative expected second derivative — is the load-bearing identity of [INFR-B3]; we use it in
@sec-an to recognize the denominator of our Taylor expansion as $I_1$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

We will also use, once, that **information adds over i.i.d. data**: the score $U_n=\sum_i u_i$
is a sum of $n$ i.i.d. mean-zero terms each with variance $I_1$, so
$\operatorname{Var}_{\theta_0}[U_n(\theta_0)]=n\,I_1(\theta_0)=I_n(\theta_0)$ (variances of
independent terms add). This is the precise sense, derived in [INFR-B3], in which *more data
is more information*.

## Consistency: the MLE finds the truth {#sec-consistency}

We make the @fig-consistency-intuition picture rigorous. The argument has two ingredients: the
LLN pins the normalized log-likelihood onto a fixed limit, and an information-theoretic
inequality places that limit's unique peak at $\theta_0$.

### The limit of the normalized log-likelihood

Define the **normalized log-likelihood** $M_n(\theta)=\frac1n\ell_n(\theta)=\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n
\log f(X_i\mid\theta)$. For each *fixed* $\theta$, this is an average of i.i.d. random variables
$\log f(X_i\mid\theta)$, so by the Law of Large Numbers ([INFR-A2], @thm-wlln there) it
converges in probability to their common expectation, taken under the truth $\theta_0$:
$$
M_n(\theta) \xrightarrow{\ P\ } M(\theta) := \mathbb E_{\theta_0}\bigl[\log f(X\mid\theta)\bigr].
$$ {#eq-Mlimit}
The limit $M(\theta)$ is a deterministic function of $\theta$ — the randomness has averaged
out. Consistency will follow if $M(\theta)$ is **uniquely maximized at $\theta_0$**, because
then the maximizer $\hat\theta_n$ of $M_n$ must converge to the maximizer $\theta_0$ of $M$.
(Turning "the maximizers converge because the functions converge" into a fully rigorous
statement needs a *uniform* law of large numbers — convergence of $M_n$ to $M$ at the same
rate across $\theta$ — which holds under the regularity conditions; we flag this as the one
technical import and concentrate on the part that carries the meaning, the location of the
peak.)

### The peak of the limit is at the truth

::: {#thm-kl}
**Theorem (the expected log-likelihood is maximized at the truth).** Under identifiability
(condition 1), $M(\theta)\le M(\theta_0)$ for every $\theta$, with equality only at
$\theta=\theta_0$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Look at the *gap* between the truth and a competitor, and show it is never negative:
$$
M(\theta_0)-M(\theta)
= \mathbb E_{\theta_0}\bigl[\log f(X\mid\theta_0)-\log f(X\mid\theta)\bigr]
= \mathbb E_{\theta_0}\!\left[\log\frac{f(X\mid\theta_0)}{f(X\mid\theta)}\right],
$$
using $\log a-\log b=\log(a/b)$. This is the **Kullback–Leibler divergence** $D(f_{\theta_0}\,\|\,
f_\theta)$ from the truth to the competitor; we now show it is $\ge 0$ with one clean inequality.
Flip the fraction inside (which flips the sign of the log) so the expectation is over the
*competitor's* ratio, the form Jensen's inequality wants:
$$
M(\theta_0)-M(\theta) = -\,\mathbb E_{\theta_0}\!\left[\log\frac{f(X\mid\theta)}{f(X\mid\theta_0)}\right].
$$
The function $\log$ is **strictly concave** (its second derivative $-1/t^2$ is negative). For
a concave function, **Jensen's inequality** says $\mathbb E[\log Y]\le \log\mathbb E[Y]$ — the
expected log is at most the log of the expectation — with equality only if $Y$ is constant.
Apply it with $Y=\dfrac{f(X\mid\theta)}{f(X\mid\theta_0)}$:
$$
\mathbb E_{\theta_0}\!\left[\log\frac{f(X\mid\theta)}{f(X\mid\theta_0)}\right]
\;\le\;
\log\,\mathbb E_{\theta_0}\!\left[\frac{f(X\mid\theta)}{f(X\mid\theta_0)}\right].
$$
Now compute that inner expectation. The expectation is under $X\sim f_{\theta_0}$, so its
density is $f(x\mid\theta_0)$, which **cancels** the denominator:
$$
\mathbb E_{\theta_0}\!\left[\frac{f(X\mid\theta)}{f(X\mid\theta_0)}\right]
= \int \frac{f(x\mid\theta)}{f(x\mid\theta_0)}\,f(x\mid\theta_0)\,dx
= \int f(x\mid\theta)\,dx = 1,
$$
the last step because $f(\cdot\mid\theta)$ is itself a density and integrates to one. (This is
where **common support**, condition 2, is used: the cancellation is only valid where
$f(x\mid\theta_0)>0$, and we need the integral of $f(\cdot\mid\theta)$ over exactly that set
to be one.) Therefore the upper bound is $\log 1 = 0$, so
$$
\mathbb E_{\theta_0}\!\left[\log\frac{f(X\mid\theta)}{f(X\mid\theta_0)}\right]\le 0
\;\Longrightarrow\;
M(\theta_0)-M(\theta) = -(\text{something}\le 0)\ \ge 0 .
$$
So $M(\theta_0)\ge M(\theta)$ for all $\theta$. Equality in Jensen requires the ratio
$f(X\mid\theta)/f(X\mid\theta_0)$ to be constant (equal to $1$, since its mean is $1$),
i.e. $f(\cdot\mid\theta)=f(\cdot\mid\theta_0)$; **identifiability** (condition 1) says that
forces $\theta=\theta_0$. Hence the maximum is unique, at the truth. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {#thm-consistency}
**Theorem (consistency of the MLE).** Under the regularity conditions, the maximum-likelihood
estimator converges in probability to the true parameter: $\hat\theta_n\xrightarrow{P}\theta_0$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Assemble the two pieces. By @eq-Mlimit (the LLN, uniformly in $\theta$), the random function
$M_n$ converges to the deterministic $M$; by @thm-kl, $M$ has a unique maximum at $\theta_0$.
The MLE $\hat\theta_n$ maximizes $M_n$ (it maximizes $\ell_n=nM_n$, and the positive factor
$n$ does not move the argmax — the [INFR-B2] log/scale invariance). When a sequence of
functions converges uniformly to a limit with a *well-separated* unique maximizer, the
sequence of maximizers converges to the limit's maximizer: any $\theta$ bounded away from
$\theta_0$ has $M(\theta)$ strictly below $M(\theta_0)$ by a fixed gap, and for large $n$ the
uniform closeness of $M_n$ to $M$ means $M_n(\theta)$ cannot exceed $M_n(\theta_0)$ there — so
the maximizer $\hat\theta_n$ is eventually trapped near $\theta_0$. Formally, for any
$\varepsilon>0$, $P(|\hat\theta_n-\theta_0|>\varepsilon)\to 0$, which is convergence in
probability. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The conceptual content is worth saying in words, because it is the *why* of the whole article:
**maximum likelihood is consistent because maximizing the average log-density is, in the
limit, the same as minimizing the KL divergence to the truth, and the truth is the unique
zero of that divergence.** The MLE chases the model closest (in KL) to the data-generating
distribution; when the true model is in the family, that closest model *is* the truth.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Consistency says the MLE converges to $\theta_0$ — but the MLE of the Normal variance is
biased. Isn't that a contradiction?"* No, and the distinction is a classic exam trap.
**Bias** is a finite-$n$ statement about the *mean* of the estimator: $\mathbb
E[\widehat{\sigma^2}]=\frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2\ne\sigma^2$. **Consistency** is a large-$n$
statement about *convergence* of the estimator itself. They are compatible because the bias
$-\sigma^2/n\to 0$: the estimator can be off-center for every finite $n$ yet still pile up on
the truth as $n\to\infty$. Consistency requires only that bias *and* variance both vanish in
the limit, which they do here. An estimator can be unbiased but inconsistent (its variance
never shrinks), or biased but consistent (the MLE of $\sigma^2$) — the two notions are
genuinely independent, and saying so crisply is the answer.
:::

## Asymptotic normality: the Taylor-and-CLT engine {#sec-an}

Now the centerpiece. We know $\hat\theta_n\to\theta_0$ (consistency); we want the *shape* and
*scale* of the error $\hat\theta_n-\theta_0$. The strategy is the one previewed in
@sec-intuition: expand the score about $\theta_0$, use that the MLE zeroes the score, and read
off the error as a ratio whose numerator the CLT handles and whose denominator the LLN handles.

::: {#thm-an}
**Theorem (asymptotic normality of the MLE).** Under the regularity conditions, with
$0<I_1(\theta_0)<\infty$,
$$
\sqrt n\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\ I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}\bigr).
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
**Step 1 — the MLE zeroes the score.** Because $\theta_0$ is interior (condition 4) and
$\hat\theta_n\to\theta_0$, for large $n$ the MLE is interior too, so it satisfies the score
equation
$$
U_n(\hat\theta_n)=0 .
$$ {#eq-an-1}

**Step 2 — Taylor-expand the score about the truth.** The score $U_n(\theta)$ is a smooth
function of $\theta$ (condition 3). Expand it about $\theta_0$ to first order, with the
remainder evaluated at some intermediate point $\tilde\theta_n$ lying between $\hat\theta_n$
and $\theta_0$ (this is the **mean-value form of Taylor's theorem** from Calc 1: for a
differentiable $g$, $g(b)=g(a)+g'(\tilde a)(b-a)$ for some $\tilde a$ between $a$ and $b$).
Applied to $U_n$ at the points $\hat\theta_n$ and $\theta_0$:
$$
U_n(\hat\theta_n) = U_n(\theta_0) + U_n'(\tilde\theta_n)\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0).
$$
The left side is $0$ by @eq-an-1. Note $U_n'=\ell_n''$, the second derivative of the
log-likelihood. So
$$
0 = U_n(\theta_0) + \ell_n''(\tilde\theta_n)\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0).
$$ {#eq-an-2}

**Step 3 — solve for the error.** Move the second term over and divide:
$$
\hat\theta_n-\theta_0 = \frac{-\,U_n(\theta_0)}{\ell_n''(\tilde\theta_n)}
= \frac{U_n(\theta_0)}{-\,\ell_n''(\tilde\theta_n)} .
$$
Now comes the key algebraic move: rescale this ratio so that the numerator and the
denominator each become an *average* of i.i.d. terms — the only form the CLT and the LLN can
act on. Do it in two explicit, reversible steps. **First**, multiply the top and bottom of the
fraction by $\frac1n$; since we multiply numerator and denominator by the same factor, the
value is unchanged:
$$
\hat\theta_n-\theta_0
= \frac{U_n(\theta_0)}{-\,\ell_n''(\tilde\theta_n)}
= \frac{\frac1n\,U_n(\theta_0)}{-\frac1n\,\ell_n''(\tilde\theta_n)} .
$$
**Second**, multiply both sides by $\sqrt n$. The right-hand side's denominator does not
involve $\sqrt n$, so the factor lands entirely on the numerator, where
$\sqrt n\cdot\frac1n=\frac1{\sqrt n}$:
$$
\sqrt n\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)
= \frac{\sqrt n\cdot\frac1n\,U_n(\theta_0)}{-\frac1n\,\ell_n''(\tilde\theta_n)}
= \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt n}\,U_n(\theta_0)}{\;-\frac1n\,\ell_n''(\tilde\theta_n)\;} .
$$ {#eq-an-3}
That is the whole trick: the $\sqrt n$ we needed got *distributed* as $\frac1{\sqrt n}$ onto
the score $U_n=\sum_i u(X_i;\theta_0)$ — the exact normalization the CLT wants for a sum of
$n$ i.i.d. terms — while the second derivative picked up the $\frac1n$ that turns it into a
sample average the LLN can collapse. We now take the numerator and denominator in turn.

**Step 4 — the numerator is asymptotically Gaussian (CLT).** The numerator is
$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt n}\,U_n(\theta_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n u(X_i;\theta_0),
$$
a normalized sum of the i.i.d. per-observation scores $u(X_i;\theta_0)$. By @lem-score-mean
each has **mean $0$**, and by @lem-score-var each has **variance $I_1(\theta_0)$**, finite and
positive by condition 6. This is precisely the setting of the Central Limit Theorem
([INFR-A2], @thm-clt): a normalized sum $\frac1{\sqrt n}\sum(\text{mean-0, var-}\sigma^2)$
converges in distribution to $\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2)$. With $\sigma^2=I_1(\theta_0)$,
$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt n}\,U_n(\theta_0)\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\ I_1(\theta_0)\bigr).
$$ {#eq-an-num}

**Step 5 — the denominator converges to the information (LLN).** The denominator is
$$
-\frac1n\,\ell_n''(\tilde\theta_n) = -\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \partial_\theta^2\log f(X_i\mid\tilde\theta_n).
$$
Consider first the same average evaluated at the truth $\theta_0$ instead of
$\tilde\theta_n$: it is an average of i.i.d. terms $-\partial_\theta^2\log f(X_i\mid\theta_0)$,
so by the LLN it converges in probability to their mean,
$\mathbb E_{\theta_0}[-\partial_\theta^2\log f(X\mid\theta_0)]$ — which by the information
equality of @lem-score-var is exactly $I_1(\theta_0)$. It remains to replace $\theta_0$ by
$\tilde\theta_n$. Since $\tilde\theta_n$ lies between $\hat\theta_n$ and $\theta_0$ and
$\hat\theta_n\xrightarrow{P}\theta_0$ (consistency), the intermediate point is squeezed:
$\tilde\theta_n\xrightarrow{P}\theta_0$. Under the smoothness condition the average is
continuous in the evaluation point uniformly enough that evaluating at $\tilde\theta_n$ rather
than $\theta_0$ does not change the limit — so
$$
-\frac1n\,\ell_n''(\tilde\theta_n)\ \xrightarrow{\ P\ }\ I_1(\theta_0).
$$ {#eq-an-den}

**Step 6 — combine with Slutsky.** We have a numerator converging in distribution to a
Gaussian (@eq-an-num) over a denominator converging in probability to the *constant*
$I_1(\theta_0)$ (@eq-an-den). **Slutsky's theorem** ([PROB-21]) is exactly the tool for a
ratio of this kind: if $A_n\xrightarrow{d}A$ and $B_n\xrightarrow{P}b$ for a nonzero constant
$b$, then $A_n/B_n\xrightarrow{d}A/b$. Here $A\sim\mathcal N(0,I_1(\theta_0))$ and
$b=I_1(\theta_0)$, so
$$
\sqrt n\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)
= \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt n}U_n(\theta_0)}{-\frac1n\ell_n''(\tilde\theta_n)}
\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\
\frac{\mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\,I_1(\theta_0)\bigr)}{I_1(\theta_0)} .
$$
Dividing a $\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2)$ variable by the constant $c$ scales its variance by
$1/c^2$ (variance of $cX$ is $c^2\operatorname{Var}X$, run backward), so the limit is
$\mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\ I_1(\theta_0)/I_1(\theta_0)^2\bigr)=\mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\
I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}\bigr)$. That is the claim. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Stand back and admire what just happened, because it is the engine room of the whole track.
**Three imported theorems did all the work, each in exactly one place:** Taylor's theorem
(Step 2) turned the implicit equation $U_n(\hat\theta_n)=0$ into something linear in the error;
the CLT (Step 4) made the numerator Gaussian, *because the score is a sum of i.i.d. terms*; the
LLN (Step 5) made the denominator a constant, *the Fisher information*; and Slutsky (Step 6)
glued the pieces. The variance came out $I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}$ for a reason you can now see
mechanically: the numerator contributed a variance of $I_1$ (the score's variance) and the
denominator divided by $I_1$ twice (once for being a denominator, squared by Slutsky) —
leaving $1/I_1$.

::: {.callout-important title="The standard error you will actually use"}
@thm-an says $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\approx\mathcal N(0,I_1^{-1})$, equivalently
$$
\hat\theta_n\ \approx\ \mathcal N\!\Bigl(\theta_0,\ \frac{1}{n\,I_1(\theta_0)}\Bigr)
= \mathcal N\!\Bigl(\theta_0,\ \frac{1}{I_n(\theta_0)}\Bigr),
$$
using $I_n=nI_1$. The **asymptotic standard error** is $\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}=1/\sqrt{n\,
I_1(\hat\theta_n)}$, where we plug in the estimate $\hat\theta_n$ for the unknown $\theta_0$
(legitimate because $I_1$ is continuous and $\hat\theta_n\to\theta_0$, one more Slutsky step).
This is the standard error behind the **Wald interval** $\hat\theta_n\pm z_{\alpha/2}\,
\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}$ of [INFR-C3] and the Wald test of [INFR-D3]. Every "$\pm$" attached to a
maximum-likelihood estimate in any software package is, under the hood, $1/\sqrt{I_n}$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In the standard error, do you use the expected information $I_1(\theta)$ or the observed
information $-\frac1n\ell_n''(\hat\theta_n)$?"* Both are valid — they have the **same limit**
by @eq-an-den — but they differ for finite $n$, and the choice has a name. Using the expected
Fisher information $I_1(\hat\theta_n)$ (the theoretical formula, with $\hat\theta$ plugged in)
gives the **expected-information** standard error; using the curvature of the actual fitted
log-likelihood, $-\ell_n''(\hat\theta_n)$, gives the **observed-information** standard error.
Efron and Hinkley (1978) argued the *observed* information is usually preferable: it reflects
the curvature of the likelihood you actually saw, conditions on the data, and tends to give
better finite-sample coverage. Most optimizers return it for free — it is the Hessian at the
optimum they already computed (the same matrix Newton's method used in [INFR-B2]). Knowing
that "the inverse Hessian at the MLE is the covariance matrix" is the practical form of this
entire theorem.
:::

## Asymptotic efficiency: the MLE hits the floor {#sec-efficiency}

We have the MLE's limiting variance, $I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}$ per $\sqrt n$, i.e.
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_n)\approx 1/I_n(\theta_0)$. Compare it to the **Cramér–Rao lower
bound** of [INFR-B3], which says that for *any* unbiased estimator $T_n$ of $\theta$,
$$
\operatorname{Var}_{\theta}(T_n)\ \ge\ \frac{1}{I_n(\theta)} = \frac{1}{n\,I_1(\theta)} .
$$
The MLE's asymptotic variance is *exactly this bound*. No regular estimator can do
asymptotically better — the MLE squeezes out all the information the model contains.

::: {#def-efficiency}
**Definition (asymptotic efficiency).** An estimator $T_n$ with $\sqrt n(T_n-\theta_0)
\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,v(\theta_0))$ is **asymptotically efficient** if its asymptotic
variance attains the Cramér–Rao floor, $v(\theta_0)=I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}$. Its **asymptotic
relative efficiency** versus another estimator $S_n$ (asymptotic variance $w$) is the ratio
$\mathrm{ARE}(T_n,S_n)=w(\theta_0)/v(\theta_0)$.
:::

::: {#thm-efficiency}
**Theorem (the MLE is asymptotically efficient).** Under the regularity conditions, the MLE
attains the Cramér–Rao bound in the limit: its asymptotic variance is $I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}$,
the smallest possible.
:::

::: {.proof}
@thm-an already computed the asymptotic variance to be $I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}$. The Cramér–Rao
theorem of [INFR-B3] states that the asymptotic variance of any regular (asymptotically
unbiased) estimator is at least $I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}$. The MLE meets that lower bound with
equality, so it is efficient by @def-efficiency. There is nothing more to prove: the
*content* of the theorem is the coincidence of the two formulas — the MLE's limiting variance,
derived in @thm-an as "score variance over information squared," equals the information's
inverse, which is precisely the floor. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The honest caveat, which separates a slogan from understanding: this is *asymptotic*
efficiency. For finite $n$ the MLE can be beaten — a biased estimator can have smaller mean
squared error (we saw the $\div n$ variance MLE do exactly this against the $\div(n-1)$
version in [INFR-B2]), and there exist "superefficient" estimators that beat the bound at
isolated points (Hodges' estimator, at the price of catastrophic behavior nearby). What is
true and clean is the limit: *asymptotically, and uniformly in a sense made precise by the
Hájek–Le Cam convolution theorem, the MLE cannot be beaten.* @fig-efficiency makes the
finite-$n$ approach to the floor visible by racing the MLE against a competitor.

```{python}
#| label: fig-efficiency
#| fig-cap: 'The MLE reaches the efficiency floor; a competitor does not. We estimate the rate $\lambda$ of an Exponential$(\lambda)$ distribution two ways from samples of growing size: the MLE $\hat\lambda=1/\bar x$ (blue), and a method-of-moments-style competitor based on the sample median, $\tilde\lambda=\log 2/\mathrm{median}$ (orange). For each $n$ we plot the simulated variance of each estimator (over $4000$ samples) times $n$, which should converge to the *asymptotic variance per* $\sqrt n$. The MLE''s curve settles onto the Cramér–Rao floor $I_1^{-1}=\lambda_0^2$ (red dashed); the median-based estimator settles *above* it — it is consistent but not efficient, leaving information on the table.'
lam0 = 1.5
ns = np.array([10, 20, 40, 80, 160, 320, 640])
reps = 4000
var_mle, var_med = [], []
for nn in ns:
    X = rng.exponential(1.0/lam0, size=(reps, nn))   # Exp with rate lam0 -> scale 1/lam0
    mle = 1.0 / X.mean(axis=1)                         # MLE: 1/xbar
    med = np.log(2) / np.median(X, axis=1)            # competitor from the median
    var_mle.append(nn * mle.var())
    var_med.append(nn * med.var())

floor = lam0**2   # I_1(lam) = 1/lam^2 for Exponential -> CRLB per-sqrt-n = lam0^2
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(ns, var_mle, "o-", color=DATA, label=r"MLE $\hat\lambda=1/\bar x$")
ax.plot(ns, var_med, "s-", color="#e07a00", label=r"median-based $\tilde\lambda$")
ax.axhline(floor, color=CURVE, ls="--", lw=1.8,
           label=fr"Cram\'er--Rao floor $I_1^{{-1}}=\lambda_0^2={floor:.2f}$")
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("sample size $n$ (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$n\times\mathrm{Var}$ (asymptotic variance)")
ax.set_title("The MLE settles onto the efficiency floor; the rival does not")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"floor (lambda0^2) = {floor:.3f}")
print(f"MLE  n*Var at n={ns[-1]}: {var_mle[-1]:.3f}")
print(f"median-based n*Var at n={ns[-1]}: {var_med[-1]:.3f}")
print(f"asymptotic relative efficiency (MLE/median) ~ {var_mle[-1]/var_med[-1]:.3f}")
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"The sample mean and the sample median both estimate the center of a symmetric distribution.
Which is more efficient, and does it matter what the distribution is?"* It depends entirely on
the distribution, and that is the point. For a **Normal** population the mean is the MLE of the
center and is efficient; the median's asymptotic variance is $\pi/2\approx1.57$ times larger,
so $\mathrm{ARE}(\text{median},\text{mean})\approx 0.64$ — the median wastes about 36% of the
information. But for a **heavy-tailed** population (Laplace/double-exponential, where the
median *is* the MLE, or anything with fat tails), the ranking flips and the median is the
efficient choice while the mean is dragged around by outliers. Efficiency is a statement about
*the model you assumed* — the MLE is efficient *for its own model*, which is exactly why
misspecification (@sec-failure) is so corrosive: it makes you efficient at fitting the wrong
thing.
:::

# The delta method: distributions of transformed estimators {#sec-delta}

Asymptotic normality is a statement about $\hat\theta_n$. But we usually want a *function* of
the parameter — the odds $p/(1-p)$ rather than $p$, the standard deviation $\sqrt{\sigma^2}$
rather than the variance, the half-life $\log 2/\lambda$ rather than the rate. By the
invariance of the MLE ([INFR-B2], @thm-invariance), the *point* estimate of $g(\theta)$ is just
$g(\hat\theta_n)$ — but what is its *standard error*? The **delta method** answers this with
one Taylor expansion, and it is the workhorse that turns @thm-an into standard errors for
everything downstream.

## The idea: linearize the function at the estimate

The transformation $g$ is generally nonlinear, so $g(\hat\theta_n)$ is a nonlinear function of
an asymptotically Gaussian variable, and a nonlinear function of a Gaussian is not Gaussian.
But $\hat\theta_n$ is *close* to $\theta_0$ (consistency), and over a tiny neighborhood any
smooth $g$ looks like its tangent line — and a *linear* function of a Gaussian *is* Gaussian.
So we replace $g$ by its tangent at $\theta_0$, and the slope of that tangent, $g'(\theta_0)$,
becomes the scale factor on the variance. @fig-delta draws exactly this linearization.

```{python}
#| label: fig-delta
#| fig-cap: 'The delta method as a tangent-line approximation. The MLE $\hat\theta_n$ (blue density on the horizontal axis) is approximately Gaussian, tightly concentrated about $\theta_0$. We push it through a nonlinear $g(\theta)=\theta^2$ (black curve). Over the narrow spread of $\hat\theta_n$, $g$ is indistinguishable from its tangent line at $\theta_0$ (red), whose slope is $g''(\theta_0)$. The induced density of $g(\hat\theta_n)$ (green, on the vertical axis) is therefore again approximately Gaussian, but with its spread *scaled by* $|g''(\theta_0)|$ — wider here because the tangent is steep. That scaling, squared, is the delta-method variance factor.'
theta0 = 1.4
se = 0.16
g = lambda t: t**2
gp = lambda t: 2*t

t = np.linspace(theta0 - 4*se, theta0 + 4*se, 300)
tang = g(theta0) + gp(theta0)*(t - theta0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 5.2))
# the function and its tangent
ax.plot(t, g(t), color="black", lw=2.0, label=r"$g(\theta)=\theta^2$")
ax.plot(t, tang, color=CURVE, lw=1.8, ls="--", label=r"tangent at $\theta_0$, slope $g'(\theta_0)$")
ax.scatter([theta0], [g(theta0)], color=ACCENT, s=55, zorder=5)

# density of theta_hat along the bottom
xb = np.linspace(theta0 - 4*se, theta0 + 4*se, 300)
dens_x = np.exp(-(xb-theta0)**2/(2*se**2)); dens_x /= dens_x.max()
base = g(theta0) - 0.9
ax.fill_between(xb, base, base + 0.5*dens_x, color=FILL, alpha=0.8)
ax.text(theta0, base - 0.18, r"density of $\hat\theta_n$", color=DATA, ha="center", fontsize=9)

# induced density of g(theta_hat) along the left
se_g = abs(gp(theta0))*se
yb = np.linspace(g(theta0) - 4*se_g, g(theta0) + 4*se_g, 300)
dens_y = np.exp(-(yb-g(theta0))**2/(2*se_g**2)); dens_y /= dens_y.max()
left = theta0 - 4*se - 0.02
ax.fill_betweenx(yb, left, left + 0.10*dens_y, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.55)
ax.text(left + 0.02, g(theta0)+3.0*se_g, r"density of $g(\hat\theta_n)$",
        color=ACCENT, fontsize=9, rotation=90, va="top")

ax.axvline(theta0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax.axhline(g(theta0), color=GREY, lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\theta$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$g(\theta)$")
ax.set_title("A smooth $g$ acts like its tangent over the MLE's spread")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9, loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The theorem, derived

::: {#thm-delta}
**Theorem (the delta method).** Suppose $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal
N(0,v)$ for some asymptotic variance $v$, and let $g$ be differentiable at $\theta_0$ with
$g'(\theta_0)\ne 0$. Then
$$
\sqrt n\,\bigl(g(\hat\theta_n)-g(\theta_0)\bigr)\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\ g'(\theta_0)^2\,v\bigr).
$$
In particular, for the MLE ($v=I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}$), the asymptotic variance of $g(\hat\theta_n)$
is $g'(\theta_0)^2\,I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Apply the mean-value form of Taylor's theorem to $g$ between $\hat\theta_n$ and $\theta_0$
(the same Calc-1 tool as in @thm-an): for some $\bar\theta_n$ between them,
$$
g(\hat\theta_n) = g(\theta_0) + g'(\bar\theta_n)\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0).
$$
Subtract $g(\theta_0)$ and multiply by $\sqrt n$:
$$
\sqrt n\,\bigl(g(\hat\theta_n)-g(\theta_0)\bigr) = g'(\bar\theta_n)\cdot\sqrt n\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0).
$$
Examine the two factors. Since $\hat\theta_n\xrightarrow{P}\theta_0$ (consistency) and
$\bar\theta_n$ is squeezed between $\hat\theta_n$ and $\theta_0$, we have
$\bar\theta_n\xrightarrow{P}\theta_0$; and $g'$ is continuous at $\theta_0$ (differentiable
there), so by the continuous-mapping idea $g'(\bar\theta_n)\xrightarrow{P}g'(\theta_0)$, a
*constant*. The other factor $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,v)$ by
hypothesis. So we have (constant-converging) $\times$ (distribution-converging), and **Slutsky's
theorem** again gives the product's limit as $g'(\theta_0)\times\mathcal N(0,v)$. Multiplying a
$\mathcal N(0,v)$ variable by the constant $g'(\theta_0)$ scales its variance by
$g'(\theta_0)^2$ (the $c^2$ rule), so
$$
\sqrt n\,\bigl(g(\hat\theta_n)-g(\theta_0)\bigr)\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\
\mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\ g'(\theta_0)^2\,v\bigr). \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

The mechanism is the same tangent-line picture as @fig-delta: $g$ acts linearly over the
narrow spread of $\hat\theta_n$, and a linear map scales a Gaussian's standard deviation by the
absolute slope (variance by the slope squared). Two immediate, constantly-used corollaries:

- **Standard error of a transformed estimate.** $\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}\bigl(g(\hat\theta_n)\bigr)
  = |g'(\hat\theta_n)|\cdot\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\hat\theta_n)$. To get the standard error of
  the odds, the half-life, or the standard deviation, multiply the parameter's standard error
  by the absolute derivative of the transformation, evaluated at the estimate. No new
  asymptotic argument.
- **Vector case.** If $\theta$ is a vector with asymptotic covariance matrix $\Sigma=I_1^{-1}$
  and $g$ is scalar-valued, the variance becomes $\nabla g(\theta_0)^\top\,\Sigma\,\nabla
  g(\theta_0)$ — the gradient replaces the derivative, and the quadratic form replaces the
  square. This is how Regression attaches a standard error to *any* smooth combination of
  fitted coefficients (a predicted value, a ratio of effects).

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The delta method dies where the derivative vanishes.** @thm-delta requires
$g'(\theta_0)\ne 0$. If the tangent is *flat* at $\theta_0$ — say $g(\theta)=\theta^2$ and the
true value is $\theta_0=0$ — the first-order term disappears, the linear approximation says
"zero variance," and the method gives a degenerate, wrong answer. The correct limiting
distribution there is **not** Gaussian: a *second-order* delta method shows $n\,g(\hat\theta_n)$
converges to a (scaled) **chi-square**, because near a flat point $g$ looks quadratic and the
square of a Gaussian is chi-square. This is not a rare curiosity — it is exactly what happens
when you test a parameter *at* a boundary or estimate a variance component that is truly zero,
and it is why naive Wald intervals can misbehave near such points (a theme of [INFR-C3]).
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why not just simulate / bootstrap the standard error of $g(\hat\theta)$ instead of using the
delta method?"* You often should, and an interviewer wants you to know the trade-off. The
delta method is **analytic, instant, and transparent** — one derivative — but it is a
*first-order* approximation: it can be poor when $g$ is sharply curved over the estimate's
spread, when $n$ is small, or near $g'=0$. The **bootstrap** ([INFR-E1]) makes no linearity
assumption and captures skewness in $g(\hat\theta_n)$'s distribution that the symmetric
Gaussian delta-method interval misses, at the cost of computation. The rule of thumb: delta
method for a quick, well-behaved transform and for *understanding* which derivative drives the
uncertainty; bootstrap when the transform is wild, the sample is small, or you do not trust the
linearization. They should agree when the delta method's assumptions hold — a useful check.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run all three properties and the delta method on real, messy problems — never a toy
rigged to flatter the theory. Each is carried end to end, and the last sits squarely on the
edge where the guarantees fail.

## Example 1 — a clinical proportion: estimate, standard error, and a transformed quantity {#sec-ex-prop}

A vaccine trial records, in one arm, how many of $n$ exposed subjects developed disease. The
data are Bernoulli($p$), $p$ the infection probability; the MLE is the sample fraction
$\hat p=k/n$ ([INFR-B2]). We attach an asymptotic standard error from @thm-an and then use the
delta method to get a standard error for a *derived* quantity epidemiologists actually report:
the **odds** $p/(1-p)$.

For the Bernoulli, the Fisher information ([INFR-B3]) is $I_1(p)=\frac{1}{p(1-p)}$, so by
@thm-an the MLE has asymptotic variance $\frac{p(1-p)}{n}$ — giving the familiar
$\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\hat p)=\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)/n}$. For the odds $g(p)=p/(1-p)$, the
derivative is $g'(p)=\frac{1}{(1-p)^2}$ (quotient rule), so by @thm-delta the odds' standard
error is $|g'(\hat p)|\cdot\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\hat p)=\frac{1}{(1-\hat p)^2}\sqrt{\hat
p(1-\hat p)/n}$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-prop
#| fig-cap: 'Bernoulli MLE with asymptotic and transformed standard errors ($n=400$, $k=58$). **Left:** the sampling distribution of $\hat p$ from $20{,}000$ simulated trials at the estimated $p$ (blue) against the asymptotic Gaussian $\mathcal N(\hat p, \hat p(1-\hat p)/n)$ from @thm-an (red) — a near-perfect match at this $n$. **Right:** the same for the *odds* $g(\hat p)=\hat p/(1-\hat p)$ (blue) against the delta-method Gaussian with SD $|g''(\hat p)|\,\mathrm{SE}(\hat p)$ (red). The delta method''s symmetric Gaussian is already slightly off in the right tail — a hint of the skewness a transform introduces, foreshadowing @sec-failure.'
n, k = 400, 58
phat = k / n
se_p = np.sqrt(phat*(1-phat)/n)
odds = phat/(1-phat)
gprime = 1.0/(1-phat)**2
se_odds = gprime * se_p

# simulate sampling distributions at the estimated p
reps = 20000
sims = rng.binomial(n, phat, size=reps) / n
sims_odds = sims/(1-sims)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
gx = np.linspace(sims.min(), sims.max(), 300)
axL.hist(sims, bins=40, density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.75, label=r"sim. $\hat p$")
axL.plot(gx, np.exp(-(gx-phat)**2/(2*se_p**2))/np.sqrt(2*np.pi*se_p**2),
         color=CURVE, label=r"$\mathcal{N}(\hat p,\,\hat p(1-\hat p)/n)$")
axL.axvline(phat, color=ACCENT, lw=1.4)
axL.set_xlabel(r"$\hat p$"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title(fr"$\hat p={phat:.3f}\pm{se_p:.3f}$"); axL.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

ox = np.linspace(sims_odds.min(), sims_odds.max(), 300)
axR.hist(sims_odds, bins=40, density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.75, label=r"sim. odds")
axR.plot(ox, np.exp(-(ox-odds)**2/(2*se_odds**2))/np.sqrt(2*np.pi*se_odds**2),
         color=CURVE, label="delta-method $\\mathcal{N}$")
axR.axvline(odds, color=ACCENT, lw=1.4)
axR.set_xlabel(r"odds $\hat p/(1-\hat p)$"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.set_title(fr"odds $={odds:.3f}\pm{se_odds:.3f}$"); axR.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"p_hat = {phat:.4f},  SE(p_hat) = {se_p:.4f}")
print(f"odds  = {odds:.4f},  delta-method SE(odds) = {se_odds:.4f}")
print(f"95% Wald CI for p:    [{phat-1.96*se_p:.4f}, {phat+1.96*se_p:.4f}]")
```

Read the output. The estimate is $\hat p=`{python} f"{phat:.3f}"`$ with asymptotic standard
error $`{python} f"{se_p:.4f}"`$, giving a Wald interval
$[`{python} f"{phat-1.96*se_p:.3f}"`,\ `{python} f"{phat+1.96*se_p:.3f}"`]$. The odds estimate
is $`{python} f"{odds:.3f}"`$ with a delta-method standard error of
$`{python} f"{se_odds:.4f}"`$, obtained by *nothing more than* multiplying $\mathrm{SE}(\hat
p)$ by $|g'(\hat p)|=1/(1-\hat p)^2$. The left panel confirms @thm-an is excellent here; the
right panel shows the delta-method approximation for the odds is good but already mildly
strained in the tail, because squaring-type transforms inject skew the Gaussian cannot
represent — the honest limit of a first-order method.

## Example 2 — an exponential lifetime: information, efficiency, and the half-life {#sec-ex-exp}

Reliability engineers model time-to-failure of a component as Exponential with rate $\lambda$
(failures per hour). The MLE is $\hat\lambda=1/\bar x$ (the reciprocal mean lifetime, by
invariance from $\hat{\mathbb E[X]}=\bar x$). The Fisher information is $I_1(\lambda)=1/\lambda^2$
([INFR-B3]), so by @thm-an the asymptotic variance of $\hat\lambda$ is $\lambda^2/n$, and its
standard error is $\hat\lambda/\sqrt n$. We also report the **median lifetime** (the "half-life"
where half the units have failed), $m=\log 2/\lambda$, a transformed quantity — its
delta-method standard error follows from $g(\lambda)=\log 2/\lambda$, $g'(\lambda)=-\log
2/\lambda^2$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-exp
#| fig-cap: 'Exponential lifetime: MLE, its asymptotic normality, and a transformed quantity ($n=120$ components, true rate $\lambda_0=0.04$/hr). **Left:** one observed lifetime sample (blue histogram) with the fitted Exponential density at $\hat\lambda$ (red). **Right:** over $15{,}000$ simulated experiments, the sampling distribution of $\sqrt n(\hat\lambda-\lambda_0)$ (blue) matched by the predicted $\mathcal N(0,\lambda_0^2)$ (red). The estimated median lifetime $\log 2/\hat\lambda$ and its delta-method standard error are printed below — a complete inference for a quantity we never directly parameterized.'
lam0 = 0.04
n = 120
# one illustrative sample
sample = rng.exponential(1/lam0, size=n)
lamhat = 1.0/sample.mean()

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
xs = np.linspace(0, sample.max(), 200)
axL.hist(sample, bins=25, density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.75, label="lifetimes")
axL.plot(xs, lamhat*np.exp(-lamhat*xs), color=CURVE,
         label=fr"fit $\hat\lambda={lamhat:.4f}$")
axL.set_xlabel("lifetime (hours)"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title("Data and fitted Exponential"); axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

# sampling distribution of sqrt(n)(lamhat - lam0)
reps = 15000
S = rng.exponential(1/lam0, size=(reps, n))
lh = 1.0/S.mean(axis=1)
z = np.sqrt(n)*(lh - lam0)
gx = np.linspace(z.min(), z.max(), 300)
av = lam0**2
axR.hist(z, bins=50, density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.75, label=r"sim. $\sqrt{n}(\hat\lambda-\lambda_0)$")
axR.plot(gx, np.exp(-gx**2/(2*av))/np.sqrt(2*np.pi*av), color=CURVE,
         label=fr"$\mathcal{{N}}(0,\lambda_0^2={av:.4f})$")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\sqrt{n}\,(\hat\lambda-\lambda_0)$"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.set_title("Asymptotic normality of the rate MLE"); axR.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

# delta method for the median lifetime m = log2 / lambda
se_lam = lamhat/np.sqrt(n)
median_hat = np.log(2)/lamhat
gprime = -np.log(2)/lamhat**2
se_median = abs(gprime)*se_lam
print(f"lambda_hat = {lamhat:.5f} /hr,  SE = {se_lam:.5f}")
print(f"median lifetime  = log2/lambda_hat = {median_hat:.2f} hr")
print(f"delta-method SE(median) = |g'|*SE(lambda) = {se_median:.2f} hr")
print(f"95% CI for median lifetime: [{median_hat-1.96*se_median:.1f}, {median_hat+1.96*se_median:.1f}] hr")
```

The estimated failure rate is $\hat\lambda=`{python} f"{lamhat:.4f}"`$ per hour, and the right
panel confirms the rescaled error is Gaussian with the predicted variance $\lambda_0^2$. The
median lifetime comes out to $`{python} f"{median_hat:.0f}"`$ hours with a delta-method
standard error of $`{python} f"{se_median:.0f}"`$ hours, a complete confidence statement for a
quantity — the half-life — that we never put in the model as a parameter. This is the practical
power of the whole article in one example: estimate in the convenient parameterization, then
*propagate* both the point estimate (invariance) and its uncertainty (delta method) to whatever
the engineer actually wants to report.

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: small $n$ and a near-boundary proportion {#sec-ex-edge}

Now the honest boundary. The asymptotic guarantees are about $n\to\infty$; here we deliberately
violate that — a *small* sample and a parameter *near the edge* of its space — and watch the
Wald interval, built on @thm-an, fail. Suppose we run $n=20$ trials and observe a rare event
$k=1$ time, so $\hat p=0.05$. The asymptotic theory still produces a standard error and a
symmetric interval, but both are lies: the true sampling distribution of $\hat p$ is discrete,
skewed, and bunched against the floor $p=0$, nothing like the smooth Gaussian @thm-an promises
*in the limit*.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: 'Where the asymptotics fail: small $n$, near-boundary $p$. With $n=20$ and true $p=0.05$, the exact sampling distribution of $\hat p=k/n$ is the discrete, sharply right-skewed Binomial (blue stems) — it cannot look Gaussian. The asymptotic Gaussian of @thm-an (red), centered at $\hat p$ with SD $\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)/n}$, badly misrepresents it, and the symmetric Wald 95% interval (orange band) spills *below zero* into impossible negative probabilities. The whole apparatus assumes large $n$ and an interior parameter; both are violated here.'
n, p_true = 20, 0.05
kvals = np.arange(0, 9)
from math import comb
pmf = np.array([comb(n, kk)*p_true**kk*(1-p_true)**(n-kk) for kk in kvals])
phat_vals = kvals/n

# Use the observed phat = 1/20 = 0.05 for the asymptotic Gaussian / Wald interval
phat = 1/n
se = np.sqrt(phat*(1-phat)/n)
gx = np.linspace(-0.10, 0.45, 400)
gauss = np.exp(-(gx-phat)**2/(2*se**2))/np.sqrt(2*np.pi*se**2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.2))
ax.stem(phat_vals, pmf/ (1/n), linefmt=DATA, markerfmt="o", basefmt=" ",
        label=r"exact dist. of $\hat p$ (Binomial)")
ax.plot(gx, gauss, color=CURVE, label=r"asymptotic $\mathcal{N}$ (Thm)")
lo, hi = phat-1.96*se, phat+1.96*se
ax.axvspan(lo, hi, color="#e07a00", alpha=0.25, label=f"Wald 95% CI [{lo:.3f}, {hi:.3f}]")
ax.axvline(0, color="black", lw=1.0, ls=":")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\hat p$"); ax.set_ylabel("density / scaled prob.")
ax.set_title("Small $n$, near-boundary $p$: the Gaussian and Wald CI break")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"phat = {phat:.3f},  Wald 95% CI = [{lo:.4f}, {hi:.4f}]")
print(f"the lower limit is {'NEGATIVE — impossible for a probability' if lo<0 else 'fine'}")
```

The Wald interval runs from $`{python} f"{phat-1.96*np.sqrt(phat*(1-phat)/n):.3f}"`$ to
$`{python} f"{phat+1.96*np.sqrt(phat*(1-phat)/n):.3f}"`$ — and its lower end is **negative**, a
nonsensical probability. Three lessons, each one the negation of a regularity condition:

- **Small $n$ breaks the approximation, not the theorem.** @thm-an is a *limit*; at $n=20$ the
  limit has not arrived, and the discrete, skewed exact distribution is the reality. The fix is
  not to abandon likelihood but to use an interval that does not assume Gaussianity — the
  **score (Wilson)** or **profile-likelihood** intervals of [INFR-C3], which respect the
  boundary and never escape $[0,1]$.
- **Near-boundary parameters are doubly dangerous.** As $p\to 0$ the true parameter approaches
  the edge of $\Theta$ (regularity condition 4 strains), the information $I_1(p)=1/[p(1-p)]$
  blows up, and the Gaussian approximation degrades fastest exactly where the symmetric
  interval most wants to cross the boundary.
- **A standard error is not a guarantee.** The software will print $\hat p\pm\mathrm{SE}$
  without complaint. The number is only as good as the conditions behind @thm-an, and here they
  do not hold. Knowing that is the entire difference between using the formula and trusting it.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The three guarantees are theorems with hypotheses. A graduate-level command means knowing
exactly which hypothesis each failure mode kills. Here is the consolidated honest map; each row
corresponds to a regularity condition from @sec-setup.

## Small samples: the limit has not arrived {#sec-small-n}

Asymptotic normality and efficiency are statements about $n\to\infty$. For small $n$ the
Gaussian approximation can be poor: the MLE may be visibly biased (the Normal variance,
[INFR-B2]), its distribution skewed (Example 3), and Wald intervals can undercover or escape
the parameter space. *Nothing is wrong with the theory* — the regime is simply outside its
promise. The remedies are the **likelihood-ratio / profile** intervals of [INFR-C3] (which use
the actual likelihood shape, not a Gaussian approximation to it) and the **bootstrap**
([INFR-E1]) (which estimates the finite-$n$ distribution directly). When $n$ is small,
*compute, do not assume*, the sampling distribution.

## Boundary parameters: the score is not zero {#sec-boundary}

The entire normality argument began with $U_n(\hat\theta_n)=0$ — an interior maximizer has zero
score. If the true parameter sits on the boundary of $\Theta$ (a variance component that is
truly zero, a mixing proportion at $0$ or $1$, the uniform $[0,\theta]$'s endpoint of
[INFR-B2]), the maximizer is a *corner*, the score there is nonzero, and the Taylor expansion is
invalid. The limiting distribution is then **not** Gaussian — it is typically a *mixture* (e.g.
a 50/50 mix of a point mass at zero and a half-normal, Chernoff's result), and standard Wald
inference is simply wrong. Recognizing "my parameter might be at a boundary" is the trigger to
abandon the off-the-shelf $\hat\theta\pm 1.96\,\mathrm{SE}$.

## Parameter-dependent support: regularity condition 2 {#sec-support}

When the data's support depends on the parameter — uniform $[0,\theta]$, shifted exponentials,
any truncation at an unknown point — we cannot differentiate under the integral, the score
identities of @sec-score-facts fail, and the whole machine seizes. Strikingly, the MLE there is
often **super-efficient**: for the uniform, $\hat\theta=\max_i X_i$ converges at rate $n$
(not $\sqrt n$!) and its limiting distribution is *exponential*, not Gaussian. Faster
convergence, but a completely different theory — a reminder that "the MLE is $\sqrt n$-normal"
is a *conditional* statement, conditional on the regularity conditions holding.

## Misspecification: efficient at fitting the wrong model {#sec-misspec}

The quietest failure, because nothing crashes (see [INFR-B2]'s extended treatment). If the
model family does not contain the truth, the MLE still converges — but to the parameter
$\theta^\star$ minimizing the KL divergence to the truth (the "least-wrong" model in the
family), *not* to a meaningful population quantity. Worse for this article: the variance is no
longer $I_1^{-1}$. The information equality of @lem-score-var (variance of score = negative
expected curvature) **relies on the model being correct**; under misspecification the two differ,
and the correct asymptotic variance is the **sandwich** $A^{-1}BA^{-1}$, where $A$ is the
expected curvature and $B$ is the score variance (White, 1982). Using the naive $I_1^{-1}$
standard error under misspecification gives confidence intervals with wrong coverage — silently.
This is why **robust ("sandwich") standard errors** exist and are the default in much of
econometrics.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The naive standard error $1/\sqrt{I_n}$ assumes the model is correct.** The clean variance
$I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}$ of @thm-an is a consequence of the information equality, which holds *only*
when $f(\cdot\mid\theta_0)$ is the true data-generating density. If you doubt the model, the
honest standard error is the sandwich estimator (which does not assume the equality), or a
bootstrap. Quoting the model-based standard error for a misspecified model is one of the most
common — and most invisible — errors in applied work.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When are the MLE's asymptotic guarantees safe to lean on, and when would you reach for
something else?"* The summary to recite:

- **Lean on them** when the model is a credible description of the data (passes a
  goodness-of-fit check), the parameter is comfortably interior, the support does not depend on
  the parameter, and $n$ is large enough that the Gaussian approximation has kicked in. Then
  $\hat\theta\pm z\,/\sqrt{I_n}$ is asymptotically optimal and you cannot do better.
- **Be cautious** with small $n$ (use profile-likelihood or bootstrap intervals), skewed
  likelihoods (Wald intervals undercover; prefer likelihood-ratio intervals, [INFR-C3]), and
  transformations through near-flat points (the second-order delta method, @sec-delta).
- **Reach for something else** when the parameter is on a boundary (mixture / Chernoff theory),
  the support depends on the parameter (different, often faster, non-Gaussian limit), or the
  model may be misspecified (sandwich standard errors, or the bootstrap, [INFR-E1]). And when
  you want uncertainty *without* trusting any parametric form at all, the bootstrap is the
  general-purpose alternative this article's asymptotics presuppose you do not need.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[INFR-B2 *Maximum Likelihood*]** with an estimator, and **[INFR-B3 *Fisher
Information*]** with a floor on precision. This article closed the Likelihood Spine's central
question — *why is the MLE good?* — by proving it is consistent, asymptotically normal with
variance $I_1^{-1}$, and asymptotically efficient (it reaches the floor), and by deriving the
delta method that propagates all of this through any smooth transformation. The track now forks
to *use* these results:

- **[INFR-C3 *Wald, Score, and Profile Intervals*]** — turns @thm-an directly into the **Wald
  interval** $\hat\theta\pm z_{\alpha/2}/\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)}$, and explains why the
  profile/score alternatives beat it exactly in the regimes where @sec-failure says Wald breaks.
- **[INFR-D3 *The Asymptotic Trinity*]** — the **Wald, score, and likelihood-ratio tests** are
  three views of the same asymptotic-normal log-likelihood; Wilks' theorem ($2\log\Lambda
  \xrightarrow{d}\chi^2$) is this article's normality applied to a difference of log-likelihoods.
- **The Regression track** consumes this wholesale: every coefficient standard error, $z$- and
  $t$-statistic, and confidence interval in a fitted GLM is @thm-an evaluated at the fitted
  $\hat\beta$, with the inverse Fisher information (the inverse Hessian at the optimum) as the
  covariance matrix.
- **[INFR-E1 *The Bootstrap*]** — the assumption-light alternative for exactly the cases where
  @sec-failure says the asymptotics cannot be trusted.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding rigor.** Fisher's 1922 and 1925 papers conjectured the efficiency of the MLE;
  the rigorous asymptotic theory under regularity conditions was assembled by **Harald Cramér**,
  *Mathematical Methods of Statistics* (1946) — the source of the "Cramér conditions" and the
  clean consistency-plus-normality treatment this article follows. **Lucien Le Cam**'s later
  work (the LAN — local asymptotic normality — framework, and the Hájek–Le Cam convolution
  theorem) is the modern, deepest statement of *in what exact sense* the MLE cannot be beaten.
- **The efficiency caveat made precise.** The **Hodges superefficiency** example and the
  **Hájek convolution / local asymptotic minimax** theorems explain why "efficient" must be
  qualified (no estimator beats the MLE *uniformly*, even if one can at isolated points). Any
  graduate text — **van der Vaart**, *Asymptotic Statistics* (1998), Chs. 5 and 8 — develops
  this carefully and is the standard modern reference for everything in this article.
- **The misspecification theory.** Halbert White, "Maximum Likelihood Estimation of
  Misspecified Models," *Econometrica* 50 (1982) — the quasi-MLE limit and the sandwich variance
  of @sec-misspec.
- **Observed vs. expected information.** Efron and Hinkley, "Assessing the Accuracy of the
  Maximum Likelihood Estimator: Observed versus Expected Fisher Information," *Biometrika* 65
  (1978) — the argument behind the "use the observed Hessian" advice of @sec-an.
- **Open threads this article deferred.** *How do these limits become honest confidence
  intervals and tests?* — [INFR-C3] and [INFR-D3]. *What if the regularity conditions fail?* —
  the bootstrap ([INFR-E1]) and boundary/mixture theory. *What if we treat $\theta$ as random?*
  — the Bayesian turn ([INFR-E4]), where the **Bernstein–von Mises theorem** shows the posterior
  is *also* asymptotically $\mathcal N(\hat\theta_n, I_n^{-1})$, so Bayesian and frequentist
  large-sample inference coincide — a striking echo of this article's central result.
